articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Mar. 9, 1912. Profiles of the pioneer generation: William Gray, close associate of Richard Allen and Absalom Jones, who ran a large business before 1800, served as one of the city's three guardians in the 1793 yellow fever epidemic and carried on an insurance company; Cyrus Bustill, a Quaker who gave the Free African Society's remaining funds to a church communion not his own; John Emory; and the 'unmatched triumvirate' of Absalom Jones, Richard Allen and James Forten. The next period brought John Bowers (active through the anti-slavery conventions of 1831-1835), the skilled San Domingo French contingent (Montiers, Rolands, Dutrieuilles, Cuyjets, Baptistes, Jacksons, Bartholomews, Augusts), Rev. John Gloucester (arrived 1807, evolved the First African Presbyterian Church from street-corner preaching), and property-holder Robert Douglass. A business coterie of over a hundred years ago is named: John Nixon, Cato Collins, James Thomas, William LeCount, Clayton Durham, James Prosser, Robert Bogle, John Matthews and Daniel Brownhill, plus the Clarkson brothers (teachers, church workers, benevolent-society managers, real estate agents) and caterer Peter Augustin, famous from about 1816.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Mar. 2, 1912. A catalogue of Black Philadelphia 'Firsts': the 1688 Germantown protest against slavery; the first Black-owned real estate in the U.S. near Front and Chestnut (per Watson's Annals); a school founded in 1750 still in existence; Pennsylvania's 1780 abolition act; the Free African Society (1787-1791), which 'evolved' Richard Allen, Absalom Jones, William Gray, James Forten and Cyrus Bustill; James Derham, first regular physician (1788); the 1793 yellow fever nursing of the city; a Black insurance company (1796); Benjamin Banneker's almanac published in Philadelphia (1792); Absalom Jones's high-grade school (1797); the first Black woman teacher employed by the Quakers (1802, name not recorded); Jones's pronunciamento against colonization; Rev. John Gloucester's organized Presbyterians (1807); a brigade of colored soldiers mustered in Independence Square in the War of 1812; the 1816 conference shaping the A.M.E. Church with Richard Allen elevated to its head; the 1817 convention against colonization; Matt Black's first military band (1818); the first anti-slavery convention of colored men fathered by Bishop Richard Allen at the instance of Hezekiah Grice of Baltimore (1830); and the first pipe organ in a colored church, St. Thomas', 1834.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Feb. 3, 1912. Old programs as historical records: a testimonial concert for singer Thomas J. Bowers at Sansom Street Hall in February 1861 (Isaiah C. Miller the only surviving promoter; supporting talent included 'Miss Sedgwick' — Mrs. Sarah Bowers-Ball — Madame Mary L. Brown, and white pianists Prof. Bachman and Cross). The first public exercises of the Institute for Colored Youth were in 1854 under Prof. Charles L. Reason (surviving scholars: Mrs. Martha Farbeaux-Minton and Mrs. Helen Appo Cook). An I.C.Y. program of May 1864 lists teachers E. D. Bassett, Sarah M. Douglass, Grace A. Mapps, Octavius V. Catto and Jacob White Jr.; the Alumni Association met at Sansom Street Hall (Raymond D. Barr presiding; addresses by John H. Smith and John Quincy Allen; eulogium on Mary L. Ayres written by Martha F. Minton, read by Caroline R. LeCount). The 1864 graduates and orations are listed in full, including James L. Smallwood, James M. Baxter, John Wesley Cromwell and an oration 'Our Alma Mater' by O. V. Catto; venerable David Bustill attended in Quaker garb.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, May 23, 1914. Mother's Day meditation on the women of the race: the 1793 yellow fever nurses credited in Richard Allen's autobiography and Carey's history; Katherine Ferguson, who started the first Sunday school in the United States (1793) and raised forty-eight children of both colors; Phillis Wheatley; the initiative wives of Richard Allen, Absalom Jones, John Gloucester, Jacob Craig and James Forten; Philadelphia's first Black woman teacher (1802); Sarah M. Douglass, mother to thousands of girls under her care; Sunday-school teachers Omeretta Barclay and Margaret Oakry; Mary Lewton, who started what became Union A.M.E. Church in 1809 and sheltered some two thousand fugitive slaves in her Paschall Alley home between 1830 and 1859; anti-slavery worker Hester Reckless; Sojourner Truth and Harriet Tubman, whose base of operations was partly Philadelphia; the Ladies Union hospital visitors of the war years; and Miss Anna M. Parsons of Philadelphia, who suggested the Mother's Day theme to Bolivar.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Feb. 22, 1913. Prompted by Rev. W. Spencer Carpenter's lecture 'The Negro in the United States Wars' (praised by Rev. Dr. Henry L. Phillips): James Forten, born near Third and Walnut, enlisted as a lad in the Revolution, was captured by the British and refused inducements to stay. Edward H. Harris of the 43rd U.S.C.T. was first Secretary of the American Negro Historical Society and a founder of Allen A.M.E. Church. Philadelphians served in the 54th and 55th Massachusetts (George E. Stephens and Frank Welsh commissioned); Thomas Hawkins and John Lawson won Congressional medals. The battle of Red Bank pitted 400 black soldiers against 1,400 Hessians. In the War of 1812 a battalion of colored men raised by Revs. Richard Allen and Absalom Jones and James Forten was mustered in Independence Square; veterans such as Charles Barclay rode in later celebrations. In the Civil War ninety men were recruited here in 24 hours, many from the Institute for Colored Youth, including O. V. Catto, Jacob C. White Jr., John Bascom, Joseph Adger, Henry Boyer Jr. and Joseph G. Anderson; fourteen regiments were recruited at Camps William Penn and Cadwalader; the Ladies Union raised funds; and Mrs. Mary Dyson of Dean Street, a member of Central Presbyterian Church, disguised her sex and fought in several battles.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Aug. 31, 1912. Occasioned by the death of Shakespearean scholar Horace Howard Furness: a tribute to his father, Rev. Dr. William H. Furness, pastor of the First Unitarian Church (Tenth and Locust), who arrived in Philadelphia about eighty years earlier when Whittier lived at what is now 910 Lombard Street editing an anti-slavery paper. Bolivar recounts the earliest white allies — Anthony Benezet, Benjamin Lay, Benjamin Franklin and Benjamin Rush (who worshipped at St. Thomas' Church) — William Ellery Channing and the Unitarian abolitionists (Sumner, Garrison, May, Phillips, Theodore Parker), and recalls St. George's Church shutting out black men and dragging Absalom Jones from his knees at prayer. Furness kept his church doors open to black and white alike, made the invocation at the National Hall memorial meeting on the day of John Brown's execution, and entertained Charles Sumner on his Philadelphia visits. After his death a meeting at Wesley Church heard John S. Durham's sketch of his life.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Mar. 7, 1914. Concluding installment on Philadelphia's Civil War regiments: the 25th U.S.C.T. left March 15, 1864 under Col. Octavius A. Scroggs for Beaufort, New Orleans and Pensacola; the 32nd under Col. George W. Baird fought at Honey Hill, Deveaux Neck, James Island, Boydkin's Mills, Camden and the occupation of Charleston; the 41st under Col. L. F. Haskell served with the Army of the Potomac from Deep Bottom to Appomattox; the 43rd under Col. Stephen B. Yeoman fought through the Richmond-Petersburg campaign; the 45th under Col. Ulysses Doubleday and the 127th under Col. Benjamin F. Tracy (later Henry Ward Beecher's counsel) completed Camp William Penn's roster. Bolivar notes many light-skinned Philadelphians served unnoticed in white regiments (Small's, Gosline's, Collis's, Lyle's, and Rush's Lancers), that the 6th and 8th Colored were among Fox's fifteen hardest-fighting regiments, and that Gen. Wagner's recent death prompted the series.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Sep. 26, 1914. On the autobiography of Rev. Jeremiah Asher, pastor of Shiloh Baptist Church, Clifton Street below South: the church's membership was largely made up of people from Petersburg, Virginia; West F. Keeling, one of the founders, paved the way for Asher's permanent pastorate (he first preached at Shiloh in 1848 en route to Washington), with colleagues John Brown (church clerk), Spencer Mitchell, Moses Willing, Makes Allmond, Edwin Johnson and Robert Ruffin. Asher introduced the silk pulpit gown, went on a fundraising mission to England, and ran a day school in the lecture room whose pupils included Richard T. Greener and Bolivar himself. Elizabeth Taylor Greenfield, the 'Black Swan,' was a Shiloh member who gave her talent to its choir, with Mrs. Lucy A. Adger and Miss Rachel Freeman among her best scholars; Addison W. Lively, the Morris family, Anna Paging, James M. Auter and Frank Guinn are also named. Asher held an all-day memorial service on the day of John Brown's execution, became chaplain of the 6th U.S.C.T., and died soon after the war from its hardships; his grandfather fought through the Revolution in a Connecticut regiment.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Apr. 27, 1912. Review of a nearly 400-page volume published in 1836 by women of the Female Anti-Slavery Society, its 2,000-copy edition paid for by sewing societies: contents include a long and masterly address of James Forten before the Female Anti-Slavery Society; Elizabeth Heyrick's pamphlet for immediate West Indian emancipation; a 74-page Bible argument against slavery; and a pamphlet on the Wesleyan Anti-Slavery Society of Philadelphia, an outgrowth of the anti-slavery conventions held in this city (Garrison, Phillips, the Unitarians, Quakers like Lucretia Mott and Samuel R. Shipley, and colored men James McC. Crummill and Robert Purvis). William Jackson of Chester County's 'Views of Slavery' is noticed, and 131 pages carry John Quincy Adams's marathon petition speech (June 16 to July 7) in the U.S. House. Meetings were held in Philadelphia to sustain Adams: at Bethel Church the moving spirits were Dr. Belfast Burton, James Forten and Frederick Hinton, and at the First (African) Presbyterian Church, Rev. Charles Gardner and Jacob C. White.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jul. 13, 1912. Bolivar recounts a conversation with a highly educated young Southern professional who knew nothing of the anti-slavery struggle: of Garrison, Lundy, Hopper, Mott, Phillips and Buffum; of the sacrifices of colored Philadelphians from 1830 to 1863; of Harriet Tubman, whose base of operations was for the most part Philadelphia and who guided over three hundred slaves to safety; of Mary Lewton's home that sheltered more than two thousand runaways; of John C. Barr's untiring energy in the same work; of Sarah Ash, Hester Reckless, Jacob C. White, Robert Purvis and Dr. Bias; of the court fights against the fugitive slave laws; of the 'Moral Reform Magazine' of 1838 and a newspaper in this city as far back as 1841; of the school of 1750 and the legal death of slavery in Pennsylvania in 1780. Bolivar closes by urging Southern colored schools to institute historical lecture courses like the American Negro Historical Society's, citing Williams, Nell, Booker T. Washington and R. R. Wright Jr., and noting the invention of ice cream before 1800 and gastronomy raised to a fine art in 1816 — all in Philadelphia.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Apr. 13, 1912. Tribute on the death of General Henry H. Bingham, Philadelphia postmaster and long-serving Congressman: as postmaster (succeeding Cornelius Waldron) he made the first Federal appointments of colored men in the city, beginning with Rev. James Underdue, pastor of Cherry Street Baptist Church, followed by Daniel P. Adger, Edward Lewis of the 8th Ward, Alfred S. Cassey of the 5th and Louis N. Bedford of the 23rd — the start of a roster of at least two hundred Black post office clerks. James Needham organized Bingham's first political club before moving to the Tax Office (the first of his race in a municipal position), passing the famous Bingham Club to marshal Daniel P. Adger, whose campaign song 'Bingham All The Time' rang from Liberty Hall, Lombard above 7th. In 1881 Bingham gave clerkships to Richard Greener(?) Warrick and Howard Dorrell(?) in the Pension Bureau and War Department; he also worked unremittingly on veterans' pensions.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Sep. 27, 1913. Review of E. R. Turner's 'The Negro in Pennsylvania,' with its bibliography compared to Siebert's 'Underground Railroad': the pioneer advocates of freedom from Daniel Pastorius in Germantown (1688) forward. About 1732 came Benjamin Lay — the hermit dwarf of Germantown, 'a giant in intellect and courage,' who once secreted a pro-slavery woman's child to illustrate the anguish of slave mothers — and Ralph Sandiford, who pleaded before the Colonial Council. Anthony Benezet joined Lay in the work; the two raised money to establish a school for colored children in 1770, still in operation on Raspberry (OCR: 'Aurora') Street near Ninth and Locust, where Benezet himself taught. His home was at Front and Chestnut, near where the first known colored real estate owner in the country lived before his advent. Benjamin Franklin, Benjamin Rush, the Shipleys, Whartons and Robert Ralston followed after the Revolutionary War; by 1780 slavery had no legal status in Pennsylvania, and some founders of the Free African Society had been educated in Benezet's school.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Feb. 7, 1914. On bibliography and Dr. Joseph Willson's 'Sketches of the Higher Classes of Colored Society in Philadelphia' (written and published by 1841 by Willson, a dentist): Turner calls it 'interesting and temperate... by one well informed and impartial,' containing more information on the social status of the Negro in Philadelphia than any other book of the whole period. Bolivar argues it rather under- than overestimates the colored people of Philadelphia in the decade between 1830 and 1840; Willson credited James Needham with putting him on the track of authentic facts. James Forten is the one man given unmistakable space, vitalized also by writers like Marryatt and Abdy; Benjamin Rush, who worshipped at St. Thomas' Church, saw the worthier side at close range. Bolivar also cites DuBois's Atlanta University lists, R. R. Wright Jr.'s 'The Pennsylvania Negro,' and notes the Turner book could even help locate the colored woman employed by the Friends as a teacher in a Philadelphia school as far back as 1802.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jul. 26, 1913. On foreign observers of the anti-slavery period — E. S. Abdy (three volumes, London 1835), Captain Marryatt (the year after) and Dr. Marshall Hall — all predisposed toward freedom, all with much to say of James Forten. The Hall book alone notes one of the most peculiar cases under the Fugitive Slave Act, that of Mary Gilmore, which happened in 1836 in this city and was an oft-told story in Bolivar's own home: a young slave-born girl without 'a particle of physical evidence of the African in her' — fair complexion, light hair, blue eyes — was brought to Philadelphia clandestinely and secreted in the home of Mrs. Mary Proctor. Traced and claimed under the law, she was first discovered by Sarah Ash, whose livelihood came from shrouding the dead; friends sprang up on the instant and David Paul Brown came forth in her legal defense. (To be continued.)

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Dec. 28, 1912. The Tribune's annual necrology: a date-by-date list of roughly ninety Black Philadelphians who died during 1912, from Ruth Ivey Temple (Jan. 15) to Charles Coldman (Dec. 25), including Corona Magun Cook, J. Gordon Baugh, Frank A. Guinn, Dr. S. Q. Howard, Alexander A. Malson, Abbie Cassey and Frederick B. Thompson. A record of the community's losses rather than of 1830s figures, but useful for dating deaths of descendants of the pioneer families (Cassey, Baugh, Guinn, Malson).

events.name: Meetings to sustain John Quincy Adams's right-of-petition fight
events.location: Bethel A.M.E. Church and First African Presbyterian Church, Philadelphia
events.description: Public meetings held in Philadelphia, both white and colored, to sustain J. Q. Adams during his June 16 - July 7, 1838 House speech on the right of petition against chattel slavery; the chain of appeals to Congress was inaugurated in Philadelphia (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Apr. 27, 1912).

events.name: Mary Gilmore fugitive slave case
events.description: A slave-born girl of entirely European appearance, brought clandestinely to Philadelphia and secreted in Mrs. Mary Proctor's home, was claimed under the Fugitive Slave Act; Sarah Ash, a shrouder of the dead, first discovered her plight and David Paul Brown undertook her legal defense (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Jul. 26, 1913, citing Dr. Marshall Hall's book).

events.name: Muster of colored battalion in Independence Square, War of 1812
events.description: A battalion of colored men recruited and mustered in Independence Square during the War of 1812; Revs. Richard Allen and Absalom Jones and James Forten were the men who raised these troops. Veterans of both races, notably Charles Barclay, rode in carriages at later celebrations (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Feb. 22, 1913; also PP_030).

events.name: Convention against colonization
events.description: 'The first secular convention was that of 1817, to fight the scheme of colonization' — the mass meeting of colored Philadelphians rejecting the American Colonization Society (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Mar. 2, 1912).

events.name: Founding of the First African Presbyterian Church
events.description: Rev. John Gloucester arrived in 1807 and 'from preaching to a mere group on the street corners, he evolved a church, and from it grew the Presbyterian Church' among colored Philadelphians (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Mar. 9, 1912; also PP_030).

events.name: Mary Lewton's Paschall Alley fugitive shelter
events.description: Mary Lewton, who started what became Union A.M.E. Church in 1809, sheltered some two thousand fugitive slaves in her Paschall Alley home between 1830 and 1859 — more, Bolivar claims, than any other house in the country (Philadelphia Tribune, May 23, 1914; also PP_038).

events.name: First pipe organ in a colored church, St. Thomas'
events.location: St. Thomas' African Episcopal Church, Philadelphia
events.description: 'The first pipe organ was in this city at St. Thomas' Church in 1834' (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Mar. 2, 1912).

events.name: Testimonial concert for Thomas J. Bowers
events.description: A testimonial concert to singer Thomas J. Bowers, a musical and financial success whose promoters included nearly all the prominent colored men of the day; Isaiah C. Miller the only promoter surviving in 1912. Supporting talent included 'Miss Sedgwick' (Mrs. Sarah Bowers-Ball) and Madame Mary L. Brown (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Feb. 3, 1912).

events.name: First public exercises of the Institute for Colored Youth
events.description: The very first program of public exercises of the Institute for Colored Youth, made up of declamations, Bible recitations in concert and short essays, while Prof. Charles L. Reason was in charge; surviving scholars in 1912 were Mrs. Martha Farbeaux-Minton and Mrs. Helen Appo Cook (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Feb. 3, 1912).

events.name: Institute for Colored Youth commencement, May 1864
events.description: I.C.Y. commencement and alumni exercises held in wartime: teachers E. D. Bassett, Sarah M. Douglass, Grace A. Mapps, Octavius V. Catto and Jacob C. White Jr.; Alumni Association presided over by Raymond D. Barr, with Caroline R. LeCount reading Martha F. Minton's eulogium on Mary L. Ayres; graduate orations by James L. Smallwood (valedictorian), James M. Baxter, Mary V. Brown, Thomas H. Boling, Elizabeth Handy, M. Gertrude Offit, Frank J. R. Jones, James H. Roberts, Margaret Masten, Harriet C. Johnson and John Wesley Cromwell; 'Our Alma Mater' by O. V. Catto; undergraduates Theophilus J. Minton, Julia A. Brice, Charles Edward Evans, John H. Davis and Toussaint L'Ouverture Martin; venerable David Bustill attending in Quaker garb (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Feb. 3, 1912).

events.name: Rev. Jeremiah Asher settles at Shiloh Baptist Church
events.location: Shiloh Baptist Church, Clifton Street, Philadelphia
events.description: Jeremiah Asher, en route to Washington, stopped in Philadelphia in 1848 and preached at Shiloh Baptist; founder West F. Keeling was mainly instrumental in securing his permanent pastorate, with colleagues John Brown (church clerk), Spencer Mitchell, Moses Willing, Makes Allmond, Edwin Johnson and Robert Ruffin (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Sep. 26, 1914).
